name: Castro de Yecla la Vieja Loop from Vitigudino
name_original: Castro Vetón de Yecla la Vieja por el Río Huebra — circular desde Vitigudino
description: Cycle 28.9 miles (46.5 km) from Vitigudino to explore the ancient Vettonian Hillfort of Yecla la Vieja and the Río Huebra.
region_intro: Road cycling around Vitigudino, a municipality in the province of Salamanca, offers diverse landscapes for cyclists. The region is characterized by its rural setting and proximity to the Arribes del Duero Natural Park, featuring deep canyons carved by the Duero River. Many routes utilize paved surfaces, providing options from gentle rides to challenging excursions with significant elevation changes. This area provides a blend of natural scenery and varied terrain suitable for road bikes. Best road cycling routes around Vitigudino The most popular road cycling route is Castro de Yecla la Vieja Loop from Vitigudino, a 28.9 miles (46.5 km) trail that takes 1 hour 59 minutes to complete. This route passes by the ancient Vettonian Hillfort of Yecla la Vieja. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is La Peñagorda Loop from Vitigudino, a moderate 27.3 miles (43.9 km) path. This route features approximately 990 feet (300 m) of elevation gain and explores the rural scenery near the Arribes del Duero. Local road cyclists also love the Pozos de Hinojo – Bogajo loop from Vitigudino, a 28.9 miles (46.6 km) trail leading through the rural landscapes of the Comarca de Vitigudino, often completed in about 2 hours 3 minutes. Road cycling around Vitigudino is defined by the dramatic canyons of the Duero River, the rural scenery of the Comarca, and varied elevation profiles. The network offers options for easy, moderate, and difficult rides, catering to different ability levels. The guide includes highly-rated routes that showcase the best of the region's landscape. More than 30 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Vitigudino's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What types of road cycling routes can I find around Vitigudino?
A: Vitigudino offers a diverse range of road cycling routes, from moderate rides exploring rural landscapes to more challenging excursions with significant elevation changes. You'll find routes suitable for various fitness levels, primarily on paved surfaces.

Q: How many road cycling routes are available in the Vitigudino area?
A: There are 9 road cycling routes documented around Vitigudino on komoot. These include 2 easy, 5 moderate, and 2 difficult options, providing choices for different skill levels.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes starting from Vitigudino?
A: Yes, several routes are circular and start from Vitigudino. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1027622913/castro-de-yecla-la-vieja-loop-from-vitigudino" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castro de Yecla la Vieja Loop from Vitigudino&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1030850453/la-penagorda-loop-from-vitigudino" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Peñagorda Loop from Vitigudino&lt;/a&gt; are popular circular options.

Q: What kind of elevation can I expect on road cycling routes in Vitigudino?
A: Elevation profiles vary significantly. Moderate routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1030850453/la-penagorda-loop-from-vitigudino" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Peñagorda Loop&lt;/a&gt; feature around 990 feet (300 m) of elevation gain. More difficult routes, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38146813" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alto de la Jana Road – Molinera Bridge loop&lt;/a&gt;, can involve over 3,400 feet (1040 m) of climbing, requiring very good fitness.

Q: What natural attractions or scenic viewpoints can I see while road cycling in Vitigudino?
A: The region is known for the dramatic canyons of the Duero River within the Arribes del Duero Natural Park. You can find stunning vistas and viewpoints along many routes. While not directly on a road cycling route, the spectacular Pozo de los Humos Waterfall is a notable natural feature in the wider area.

Q: Are there any historical sites or landmarks along the road cycling routes?
A: Yes, some routes incorporate historical sites. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1027622913/castro-de-yecla-la-vieja-loop-from-vitigudino" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castro de Yecla la Vieja Loop from Vitigudino&lt;/a&gt; passes by the ancient &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5446366" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Vettonian Hillfort of Yecla la Vieja&lt;/a&gt;, offering a glimpse into the region's past.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Vitigudino?
A: The region generally offers good conditions for cycling for much of the year. Spring and autumn typically provide pleasant temperatures and beautiful scenery. Summers can be hot, especially in July and August, while winters are milder but can have cooler, wetter periods.

Q: Are there any long-distance road cycling routes in the area?
A: Yes, for those seeking longer rides, routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38146813" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alto de la Jana Road – Molinera Bridge loop from Valderrodrigo&lt;/a&gt; cover approximately 52.7 miles (84.8 km), offering a significant challenge and duration.

Q: Can I find family-friendly road cycling routes in Vitigudino?
A: While specific 'family-friendly' routes are not explicitly categorized, the region offers easier, shorter rides with minimal elevation gain that could be suitable for families with older children or those looking for a relaxed pace. Always check the route's difficulty and elevation profile before setting out.

Q: What are the road surfaces like on the cycling routes around Vitigudino?
A: The road cycling routes around Vitigudino are primarily on paved surfaces, making them well-suited for road bikes. However, some challenging excursions might occasionally include very short unpaved segments.

Q: What do other cyclists say about road cycling in Vitigudino?
A: More than 30 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Vitigudino's varied terrain. They often praise the blend of natural scenery, the dramatic canyons of the Duero River, and the diverse terrain that caters to different ability levels.
tips: The castro of Yecla la Vieja, also known as "El Castillo", is located a couple of kilometers south of the town of Yecla and constitutes one of the most spectacular pre-Roman fortifications on the Iberian Peninsula. It is a long-occupied town, perhaps originally called Virlanga.
It is worth a stop at the bridge and contemplate this old mill and the landscape that it shapes in the Yeltes river

Pozos de Hinojo is a municipality in the Tierra de Vitigudino region, in the province of Salamanca. It has beautiful landscapes of pastureland with abundant holm oaks and oaks and the highest nearby level is Mount Alcornoque.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start the "Castro Vetón de Yecla la Vieja by the Río Huebra — circular from Vitigudino" route?
faqs.answer: As this is a circular route starting from Vitigudino, you can typically find parking within the town itself. While specific trailhead parking isn't detailed, Vitigudino offers various public parking options suitable for starting your cycling tour. Look for available spots in the town center or residential areas.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The Salamanca province generally offers pleasant cycling conditions outside of the peak summer heat. Spring (April-May) and autumn (September-October) are ideal, providing comfortable temperatures and beautiful scenery. During these seasons, you'll avoid the intense heat of summer and the colder, potentially wetter conditions of winter.

faqs.question: Is this road cycling route suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: This route is classified as 'moderate' in difficulty. While it features 'empty roads and gentle climbs' characteristic of the region, there will be undulating or hilly sections, especially when approaching river valleys like the Huebra. Road surfaces are generally well-paved, but smaller local roads might have varying conditions. Beginners with some cycling experience should manage, but those new to road cycling might find some climbs challenging.

faqs.question: What historical sites or landmarks can I see along the route?
faqs.answer: The route is rich in historical and natural scenery. A primary draw is the Vettonian Hillfort of Yecla la Vieja, an impressive pre-Roman settlement with a dry granite wall and ancient cave engravings. You'll also pass by the Old Rio Yeltes Mill and the settlement of Pozos de Hinojo, offering glimpses into the region's past and present.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain will I encounter on this cycling tour?
faqs.answer: You can expect varied terrain. Sections will take you through flatter cereal fields, interspersed with more undulating or hilly areas, particularly near the Río Huebra and Arroyo Varlaña. The route traverses typical 'dehesa' landscapes, characterized by open grasslands dotted with holm oaks and cork oaks. Road surfaces are generally good on main roads, but smaller local roads may vary.

faqs.question: Are dogs allowed on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: While the route itself is on public roads and paths, the specific rules for dogs at the Vettonian Hillfort of Yecla la Vieja or other historical sites should be checked directly with the site management. Generally, dogs on a leash are permitted in most outdoor public areas in Spain, but always be mindful of local regulations and other cyclists.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or pay entrance fees for this cycling tour?
faqs.answer: No, there are no permits or entrance fees required to cycle this route, as it primarily uses public roads and paths. However, if you plan to visit the Archaeological Classroom of the Castro de Yecla la Vieja in the town of Yecla de Yeltes, there might be a small entrance fee for the museum itself.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with other cycling paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, while primarily a unique circular route, it does briefly intersect with several local paths. You'll find short sections that overlap with paths such as Camino de Guadramiro, Camino del Coche, Calle Vuelta de los prados, Camino a Vitigudino, and Calle Cañada, among others. These are generally local roads or tracks.

faqs.question: What should I pack for this road cycling tour?
faqs.answer: Given the moderate difficulty and varied terrain, it's advisable to pack standard road cycling gear: a helmet, appropriate cycling attire, water bottles, snacks, a basic repair kit (spare tube, pump, tire levers), and navigation tools. Sun protection (sunscreen, sunglasses) is also recommended, especially during sunnier months. Consider bringing a light jacket for cooler mornings or descents.

faqs.question: Are there any amenities like cafes or restaurants along the route?
faqs.answer: The route starts and ends in Vitigudino, where you will find various amenities including cafes, restaurants, and accommodation options. The town of Yecla de Yeltes, near the Vettonian Hillfort of Yecla la Vieja, may also offer limited services. However, for much of the route, especially through the 'dehesa' landscapes, services will be sparse, so plan to carry sufficient water and snacks.

faqs.question: What kind of natural scenery and wildlife can I expect to see?
faqs.answer: The route offers beautiful natural scenery, primarily characterized by the 'dehesa' landscape with its open grasslands and scattered holm oaks and cork oaks. You'll also enjoy scenic stretches alongside the Río Huebra and Arroyo Varlaña. While specific wildlife sightings aren't guaranteed, the region is known for its cattle-raising, and you might spot local birds or other small animals typical of Spanish rural areas.

waypoints.name: Vettonian Hillfort of Yecla la Vieja
